# (PART\*) Spatial Patterns {.unnumbered}

# Spatial Weights {#spatial_weights}

## Introduction

![](images/banners/banner-spatial-weights.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand what spatial weights represent.
2.  Understand how spatial weights are constructed (i.e., contiguity, distance).
3.  Understand how spatial weights underpin analysis of spatial patterns.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To achieve this, we'll demonstrate construction of spatial weights using country Polygons and assess neighbourhood effects on cultural similarity, using the Cultural Fixation Index (CFST) from @muthukrishna2020.

### Data {.unnumbered}

-   Natural Earth 1:10m Cultural Vectors, available in [`data/natural-earth`] [[Source](https://www.naturalearthdata.com/downloads/10m-cultural-vectors/)]
-   Cultural Distance matrix, produced for 80 country pairs by @muthukrishna2020, available in [`data/cultural-similarity`] [[Source](https://world.culturalytics.com/)]
-   Ordnance Survey (OS) 100 km grid, available in [`data/os/grids`] [[Source](https://github.com/OrdnanceSurvey/osbng-grids)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Generate Spatial Weights Matrix](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/generate-spatial-weights-matrix.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics), [Convert Spatial Weights Matrix to Table](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/convert-spatial-weights-matrix-to-table.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics), [Introduction to Neighborhood Explorer](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/introduction-to-neighborhood-explorer.html), [Add Geometry Attributes](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/add-geometry-attributes.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [Join Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [XY To Line](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/xy-to-line.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [Delete Identical](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/delete-identical.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [Summary Statistics](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/summary-statistics.html?tabs=dialog) (Analysis Tools), [Create Thiessen Polygons](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/create-thiessen-polygons.html?tabs=dialog) (Analysis Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

::: task
To begin, a conceptual discussion of spatial weights.
:::

### Spatial weights using a grid

[1] Open ArcGIS Pro

[2] Load OS grid data [`osbng_grid_100km.shp`]. This is a very simple dataset, so easy to see the effects of the various neighbourhood settings, before moving on to a more complex dataset

[3] Use [Neighbourhood Explorer](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/introduction-to-neighborhood-explorer.html) to visualise different spatial weights settings.

::: task
To discuss, neighbourhood types (distance-based, contiguity)
:::

### Spatial weights using country Polygons

[4] Load country data [`ne_10m_admin_0_countries.shp`] and repeat process.

[5] The above allows us to dynamically view connections for a single feature of interest (Select Focal Feature). To generate these connections for the entire dataset, we can use [Generate Spatial Weights Matrix](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/generate-spatial-weights-matrix.html?tabs=dialog) - talk through different settings, using a unique ID (NE_ID)

[6] Return to the Neighbourhood Explorer, where we can now use our created `swm` file as an input.

[7] To visualise all relationships, we need to use [Convert Spatial Weights Matrix to Table](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/convert-spatial-weights-matrix-to-table.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics)

[8] The resulting table contains the IDs of the start countries (`NE_ID`) and the end countries (`NID`) i.e., $UK = 78$, $Ireland = 77$, but no corresponding geometry

[9] Run [Add Geometry Attributes](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/add-geometry-attributes.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools) to Natural Earth data (centroid x and y)

[10] Then use [Join Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog) based on ID: 

- Input = swm 
- Input Field = NE_ID
- Join table = natural_earth 
- Join field = NE_ID 
- Use Field Mapping: 
  - Remove all but centroid_x, centroid_y and ADMIN 
  - Double click to rename these as source_x, source_y, source_admin 
- Validate and run

*Note* we are including the `ADMIN` attribute here, as we will use this later to link to cultural distance index

[11] Then repeat for the target locations, which is as simple as changing the input file = NID, and to target_x, target_y and target_admin

[12] Now we can use [XY To Line](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/xy-to-line.html?tabs=dialog), using source_xy and target_xy, "and preserve attributes" (all other settings as default)

[13] Engage critically with visualisation e.g., France? (centroid location is outside of the contiguous part of France, due to S. America)

### Comparison with cultural distance

[14] Open the matrix of cultural distance [`cultural-distance.csv`] which I've edited to leave only unique combinations (using "-".join(sorted([!country_1!, !country_2!]))), as summarised in `join_key`. Our task is to match those to the data created above i.e.,

::: question
Are 'neighbouring' countries (as defined by the swm), more culturally similar?
:::

*Note* the smaller the cultural distance value, the more similiar the countries are e.g., Egypt and Norway are dissimiliar (0.5953), whereas Australia and the UK are highly similar (0.0319)

[15] This csv is not editable so Export Table, and save in a gdb i.e., `practical-2.gdb`. Remove the original csv from ArcGIS to avoid confusion

::: task
To do, discuss geodatabases? 
:::

[16] Next use [Delete Identical](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/delete-identical.html?tabs=dialog), with the XY to Line layer created in [12], with Fields = Shape, to remove comparable geometries e.g., (IRE-UK, UK-IRE, N=344)

[17] Create a `target_key` in the XY to Lines dataset via Attribute Table -\> Calculate Field. Equation: "-".join(sorted([!Source_ADM!, !Target_ADM!]))

[18] Using [Join Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog), with the **editable** cultural distance (gdb) dataset as input: 

- Input Field = `join_key` (created by MT) 
- Join Table = cultural-distance 
- Join Field = `target_key` (created in [17]) 
- Transfer Fields = `target_key`

*Note* if there is a match, the `target_key` field will have data, else `<Null>`

[19] Calculate Field (distance_table): `neighbours` (text): "false" if !target_key! is None else "true"

*Note* I originally had this as "Non-bordering" and "Bordering" but this could be misleading. This is only correct if a contiguity-based SWM was created, and not a distance-based rule

[20] Finally, use [Summary Statistics] to outline results: 

- Statistic Fields = distance [Mean, SD] 
- Case Fields = neighbours

### Results

[21] Inspect output of Summary Statistics

-   Neighbouring country distance mean = 0.07, SD = 0.05 (n=85)

*Note* This is for where countries are neighbouring (as defined by the swm) AND there is a cultural distance value i.e., UK-Ireland not in the dataset.

-   Non-neighbouring country distance mean = 0.14, SD = 0.08 (n=3075)

[22] Interpretation: non-neighbouring countries are characterised (on average) by greater cultural distance (0.14 vs. 0.07, 2x). However, we can be critical here: 

- Extreme un-eveness of sample sizes 
- Overlap with SD (t-test for significance?)
- Sensitive to our designation of neighbourhood-ness 
- Sensitive to calculation of cultural similarity/distance. Can this be expressed well with a single value?

## Extra

We've been working with Polygon data, but what would we do with Points?

[23] Load the Cities data from Practical 1 [`data/esri/World_Cities.shp`] and use the Neighbourhood Explorer again, where we can use fixed distances, k-nearest neighbours, Trimmed Delaunay Triangulation

[24] Use [Create Thiessen Polygons](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/create-thiessen-polygons.html?tabs=dialog) to establish a neighbourhood network, and use Neighbourhood Explorer (Contiguity Edges Corners). This is an approach I've used in my own work, see @tomkins2021.

::: task
This will be useful for A1, as students will be working with a Point dataset. Include a subtle hint here? 
:::

## Resources {#sw_resources}

-   Tobler, W. ([1970](https://doi.org/10.2307/143141)). A Computer Movie Simulating Urban Growth in the Detroit Region. Economic Geography 46, 234–40.
-   Miller, H.J. ([2004](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8306.2004.09402005.x)). Tobler's first law and spatial analysis. Annals of the association of American geographers, 94(2), pp.284-289.
-   Tobler, W. ([2004](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8306.2004.09402009.x)). On the first law of geography: A reply. Annals of the association of American geographers, 94(2), pp.304-310.
-   Anselin, L. and Li, X. ([2020](https://doi.org/10.1111/gean.12237)). Tobler’s law in a multivariate world. Geographical Analysis, 52(4), pp.494-510.
